home.title: Game Programmer. Maker. Problem Solver.
home.intro1: From gameplay prototypes to handcrafted <kumiko>Kumiko panels</kumiko> and <keyboard>split ergonomic keyboards</keyboard> —
home.intro2: I build things that work beautifully.
home.intro3: I pivoted from leading teams in hospitality and public service into the world of game development, earning fluency in <cpp>C++</cpp>, <csharp>C#</csharp>, proficiency working in both <unity>Unity</unity> and <unreal>Unreal</unreal> at Games Academy Berlin. Now, I apply precision and creativity across digital and physical projects that challenge both logic and design.
home.explore: 🕹️ <projects>Explore</projects> semester games that simulate real studio workflows.
home.peek: 🔧 <keyboard>Peek behind the curtain</keyboard> into hardware builds and custom electronics.
home.discover: 🧗 <climbing>Discover</climbing> how climbing, coding, and crafting converge.

about.title: About Me
about.subtitle: Game programmer, maker, and perpetual tinkerer, fascinated by systems and the process of creation.
about.intro: I’m a game programmer who loves building things — whether that’s a gameplay system, a hand-cut <kumiko>Kumiko</kumiko> panel, or a custom <keyboard>Bluetooth keyboards</keyboard>. For me, the joy is in seeing something move from concept to working reality, understanding the systems beneath the surface, and refining them until they feel effortless.
about.background: Before moving into game development, I spent several years leading teams in hospitality and public service in the UK. Later, I relocated to Germany, where I had the chance to enjoy my daughter’s early years while learning German to a C1 proficiency and retraining in a new field. That period of change taught me a lot about patience, adaptability, and the value of continuous learning — qualities that underpin everything I build now.
about.training: I trained at <ga>Games Academy</ga> Berlin, focusing on C# C++, Unity and Unreal, learning not only how to write clean, performant code but also how to think like a developer in a multidisciplinary team. That environment taught me how to connect technical work with creative goals — to make code serve the feel and flow of play.
about.hobbies: Outside of programming, I’ve always kept my hands busy. Designing and fabricating custom hardware (like my wireless per-key RGB keyboard) and crafting Kumiko joinery have become creative outlets that strengthen my attention to precision and design thinking. Both disciplines — digital and physical — share the same heartbeat: iteration, structure, and a quiet respect for detail.
about.motivation: I’m motivated by curiosity and the challenge of making systems — mechanical, digital, or human — work beautifully together. In a team, I bring patience, clarity, and a genuine enthusiasm for seeing others’ ideas take shape. Whether it’s gameplay logic or joinery geometry, I love the process of solving problems collaboratively and making something that feels right.
about.curiosity: I build things because I’m endlessly curious about how the world works — how different systems, whether in design, mechanics, electronics, or code, interact and create meaningful results. I see every project as an opportunity to learn new skills, refine existing ones, and grow through the people, experiences, and ideas I encounter along the way. For me, building isn’t just about making; it’s about understanding, experimenting, and enjoying the process as much as the outcome.

contact.title: Let's Connect
contact.subtitle: Looking for the next big problem to solve. If you need a passionate engineer for your team or project, let’s connect.

projects.title: Projects
projects.subtitle: Current and previous selected Game projects. Additional projects can be found on my <github>GitHub</github>.

kyoto-conflict.title: Kyoto Conflict
kyoto-conflict.shortDescription: A fast-paced online FPS set in a futuristic Japanese suburb, blending advanced movement mechanics with tactical capture-and-defend gameplay. Built in Unreal Engine 5.5.
kyoto-conflict.description: Step into the pulsating heart of a futuristic Japanese suburb where neon-lit streets and advanced technology blend seamlessly with ancient traditions. Kyoto Conflict is an online-only first-person shooter that thrusts players into a brutal fight for dominance over a coveted artifact—a helmet of immense power capable of turning the tide of battle. Players must either storm enemy strongholds in daring raids or defend their sacred temple against relentless assault. The game features dynamic movement with bunny hopping mechanics, a unique capture/defend game mode combining Capture the Flag with attacker/defender gameplay, and strategic FOB capture points.
kyoto-conflict.team: Developed by a Games Academy student team over 8 weeks in 2024/25, following the vision of Marcel Pospiech. As one of three programmers working under Marc Hoffmann (Lead Programmer) alongside Nils Hähndel, I collaborated with 3 producers, 6 artists, 1 game designer, and numerous external specialists to create this fast-paced multiplayer FPS experience.

contributions.title: Advanced Movement System
contributions.description: Implemented fluid first-person movement mechanics...

contributions.title: Multiplayer Respawn Systems
contributions.description: Developed robust respawn mechanics...

contributions.title: User Interface Design
contributions.description: Created comprehensive UI systems...

kyoto-conflict.reflections: Koyoto Conflict was a challenging and rewarding learning experience that tested both my technical skills and collaborative abilities. I focused on gameplay development, implementing the core game loop, multiplayer replication, player interactions, and later taking full responsibility for audio integration. Working with both internal and external team members, I ensured key gameplay systems were synchronized across the network and that interactions, pickups, and spawn points functioned reliably. One major challenge was balancing project needs with personal learning goals. I adapted to an external programmer’s Blueprint-only approach to maintain compatibility, which sometimes conflicted with my goal of working in C++ to improve structured and extendable coding practices. Additionally, late-stage responsibilities for audio integration introduced unforeseen technical complications due to unfamiliar plugins in a multiplayer context. The project also highlighted the impact of weak pre-production, emphasizing the importance of early alignment, a clear design plan, and establishing a centralized knowledge base. Reflecting on the project, I learned the value of strong planning, adaptability, and collaboration. Supporting colleagues, mentoring when possible, and prioritizing effectively in the face of shifting responsibilities strengthened the team and enhanced the overall project outcome. Despite the challenges, I am proud of the systems I implemented, the teamwork fostered, and the lessons learned, which will guide my approach to future projects.

steambrawl.title: SteamBrawl
steambrawl.shortDescription: An 8-player online auto-battler set in a post-apocalyptic steampunk world, where players recruit, upgrade, and position units before clashing in tactical duels. Built in Unreal Engine 5.4 over 10 weeks.
steambrawl.description: SteamBrawl is a tactical online multiplayer auto-battler set in a post-apocalyptic steampunk world. Playable with 2-8 players (8 being ideal), players take the role of combat factions in an epic contest. Through a round-based system, they buy, upgrade, and place combat units, duel other players, with winners advancing to a grand Battle Royale at the end of each round. The game continues until only one player remains standing, combining strategic unit management with intense multiplayer competition.
steambrawl.team: Developed by an 8-person Games Academy student team over 10 weeks. As one of two programmers alongside Marc Hoffmann who took the "Lead" role, I worked with 2 artists, 2 game designers, and 2 producers, plus an external composer, to create this ambitious multiplayer auto-battler from concept to completion.

contributions.title: AI Movement & Combat Logic
contributions.description: Implemented sophisticated AI systems for unit behavior, pathfinding, and combat decision-making, ensuring units could navigate the battlefield intelligently and engage in tactical combat scenarios.

contributions.title: User Interface Programming
contributions.description: Developed comprehensive UI systems for player faction management, unit purchasing and upgrading interfaces, match progression displays, and real-time battle status indicators across the complex multiplayer experience.

contributions.title: VFX Integration
contributions.description: Integrated visual effects systems throughout the game, from unit abilities and combat impacts to environmental steampunk atmosphere effects, enhancing the post-apocalyptic aesthetic and player feedback.

contributions.title: Result Computation Systems
contributions.description: Built robust systems for calculating battle outcomes, tracking player progression through rounds, managing Battle Royale mechanics, and determining final victory conditions across the 8-player competitive structure.

steambrawl.reflections: Steambrawl was a rewarding learning experience that tested both my technical skills and collaborative abilities. I contributed to UI production, AI movement and combat logic, animation, visual effects, audio integration, and battle result calculations, quickly adapting to Unreal Engine and Blueprints. One major challenge was managing time and scope, especially with late-arriving assets, while also navigating self-doubt and refining how I communicated ideas within the team. Reflecting on the project, I learned the importance of early planning, structured communication, and leveraging available resources to accelerate learning. I’m proud of the technical contributions I made and the collaborative environment we maintained, and I carry these lessons forward to future projects.

prophecy-of-ash.title: Prophecy of Ash
prophecy-of-ash.shortDescription: A third-person adventure set in the desert canyons of Solmora, where players battle corrupted creatures and explore the last refuge of the desert elves. Built in Unreal Engine 5.5 over 8 weeks.
prophecy-of-ash.description: Prophecy of Ash is set within the vast desert canyons of Solmora — the final refuge of the elven races after their lands were consumed by a spreading corruption. Take the role of an emergent hero in the Last Refuge, explore a map filled with dangerous foes, and develop your magical and melee prowess to stem the tide of corruption. The game features tactical real-time combat, level and equipment-based progression, a main quest with multiple stages, and ten different side quests, each with their own sub-stages.
prophecy-of-ash.team: Created by Games Academy students over 8 weeks with a large multidisciplinary team including 3 producers, 4 programmers, 9 artists, plus external audio and support specialists. As one of the programmers on this ambitious project, I worked alongside Max Heinze (Lead Programmer), Marcel Jahn (Quest, Sound), and Niclas Rummler (Dialogue System) to bring this desert fantasy adventure to life.

contributions.title: Environment Scripting
contributions.description: Developed interactive environmental systems and scripted dynamic elements throughout the desert canyons and oases, creating immersive exploration experiences within the vast world of Solmora.

contributions.title: Dialogue Logic
contributions.description: Implemented dialogue tree logic and character interaction systems, working closely with the narrative team to ensure smooth story delivery and player choice integration.

contributions.title: Interaction Systems
contributions.description: Created robust player interaction frameworks for NPCs, objects, and quest elements, enabling seamless gameplay flow between exploration, combat, and story progression.

prophecy-of-ash.reflections:
Prophecy of Ash was an ambitious and highly demanding learning experience that tested both my technical and leadership skills. As the lead programmer and vision keeper, I was responsible for guiding the programming team, implementing AI, gameplay systems, and ensuring a robust and scalable core loop. I also coached less experienced programmers, providing daily support and knowledge transfer while maintaining active development on the game’s key systems.

One major challenge was navigating a lack of design clarity and limited production planning. Incomplete feature definitions, unreliable design input, and outsourced components forced me to prioritize development and problem-solving under tight deadlines. Managing these responsibilities on top of technical implementation created significant stress, particularly when integrating complex systems like enemy scaling, loot, and the Day-Night cycle.

Reflecting on the project, I learned the critical importance of clear communication, proactive planning, and documenting decisions. Supporting my team and maintaining cohesion amid a large, diverse group strengthened my leadership and mentoring abilities. Despite the challenges, I am proud of delivering a functional vertical slice with a playable core loop, solid technical foundation, and coherent game systems, and I carry these lessons forward to improve efficiency and collaboration in future projects.

trippy-stargnome.title: Trippy Stargnome
trippy-stargnome.shortDescription: A psychedelic 3D sci-fi rail shooter where players battle waves of alien invaders, upgrading their arsenal through kill streaks to liberate their home planet.
trippy-stargnome.description: Trippy Stargnome is a fast-paced, 3D sci-fi rail shooter where players blast through vibrant, surreal landscapes on a mission to repel waves of alien invaders. While the player's path through the level is tethered to a predefined route, movement remains free within the rail's boundaries, allowing for precise aiming, evasive maneuvers, and obstacle avoidance. Gameplay combines quick reflexes with tactical weapon use, offering a diverse arsenal including rapid-fire projectiles, guided missiles, and high-energy lasers. Levels feature dynamic enemy formations, environmental hazards, and collectible power-ups to keep the action unpredictable and engaging.
trippy-stargnome.team: Developed by a 7-person team: one producer, three designers, two artists, and myself as the sole programmer. My work encompassed all gameplay programming, UI design and implementation, AI systems, audio integration, and gameplay polish.

contributions.title: Waypoint-Based Rail System
contributions.description: Engineered a fully editable waypoint system, enabling level designers to easily define and adjust the player's route within the Unreal Engine editor. This approach streamlined iteration and allowed for rapid level design experimentation.

contributions.title: Dynamic Spawn System
contributions.description: Built a flexible spawn system supporting random and fixed enemy positions along the flight path. Iterated multiple versions to balance pacing, challenge, and visual performance.

contributions.title: Combat Systems
contributions.description: Developed a modular weapon framework supporting multiple weapon types, from projectiles to guided missiles and lasers, each with distinct firing mechanics and effects. Implemented power-up collection and integration into combat flow.

contributions.title: User Interface
contributions.description: Designed and implemented responsive UI elements for player health, ammunition, score, and kill streak tracking, ensuring clear and intuitive player feedback during intense gameplay moments.

contributions.title: Audio Integration
contributions.description: Implemented adaptive audio cues and effects for weapons, enemies, and environmental events, enhancing immersion and player situational awareness.

contributions.title: Technical Support & Git Management
contributions.description: Maintained the project’s Git workflow and provided technical support to the team, resolving issues with builds, version control, and Unreal Engine project setup.

contributions.title: Visual Effects & Shaders
contributions.description: Explored and implemented visual effects using Shader Graph and particle systems to enhance the psychedelic aesthetic of the world and weapon interactions.

trippy-stargnome.reflections:
Trippy Stargnome was an incredible learning experience that tested both my technical and collaborative abilities. As the sole programmer, I was responsible for implementing all gameplay systems, from player movement and weapon mechanics to enemy spawning and UI functionality. Our team managed time effectively, avoiding crunch by maintaining strong communication and clear deliverables before each gate.

One major challenge was managing project scope — balancing creativity and feasibility with our available resources and experience. While we faced interpersonal challenges and occasional conflict over design directions, we navigated these by focusing on collaboration and maintaining respect for one another’s expertise. If I could approach the project again, I’d apply a stronger leadership role in resolving creative disputes early and focus more on performance optimization from the outset.

The final product showcased our teamwork and ambition, earning us the opportunity to present at the Goldmaster Live event — a major highlight and affirmation of our efforts. I’m proud of what we achieved and grateful for the lessons learned, which I’ll carry forward into future projects.

lola.title: Lola
lola.shortDescription: A stylized exploration prototype focused on emotional storytelling, puzzles, and environmental design.
lola.description: Lola is a 2.5D side-scrolling puzzle adventure following a lost bunny navigating a dreamlike and sometimes nightmarish world. Players guide Lola through levels, using abilities such as jumping, climbing, picking up, carrying, and dropping objects, while collecting key elements to progress. The game combines narrative, puzzle-solving, and exploration in a stylized 3D environment.
lola.team: Lola was developed by a small team of two over the course of 3 weeks: Julia, responsible for design, art, animation, and asset creation, and myself, handling programming, gameplay logic, UI and menu structures, audio integration, and animation implementation. The team collaborated closely to ensure cohesive gameplay and visual storytelling.

contributions.title: Gameplay Systems
contributions.description: Implemented core gameplay mechanics including movement, climbing, object interaction, and level progression logic, ensuring responsive and intuitive controls.

contributions.title: UI & Menu Systems
contributions.description: Designed and programmed in-game UI and menu structures to provide clear player feedback and navigation.

contributions.title: Audio & Animation Integration
contributions.description: Integrated sound effects, background music, and character animations into gameplay, ensuring cohesive audiovisual feedback and immersion.

contributions.title: Puzzle & Object Interactions
contributions.description: Developed systems for object pickup, carry, and placement mechanics, enabling puzzles and progression throughout the levels.

lost-keepers.title: Lost Keepers
lost-keepers.shortDescription: A stylized 3D platform adventure inspired by Zelda, focused on environmental puzzles and exploration. Currently in development in Unreal Engine 5.6.
lost-keepers.description: Lost Keepers is a stylized 3D platform adventure inspired by Zelda, focusing on exploration, environmental puzzles, and player-driven discovery. Players follow the protagonist, Lyra, as she navigates a dungeon castle environment, using “Ghost” artifacts—magical companions like Kealen, a shield—to interact with the world and solve intricate puzzles. Each artifact provides unique abilities that are essential for progression, encouraging creative problem-solving and strategic use of tools. Currently in development using Unreal Engine 5.6, the project is a collaborative effort among friends, acquaintances, and fellow students, combining narrative, puzzle design, and stylized visuals into an engaging adventure.
lost-keepers.team: Lost Keepers is primarily developed by a small core team of 4–5 collaborators, all contributing part-time alongside other roles and responsibilities. Each team member focuses on specific areas such as narrative, art, level design, or puzzle creation, while I serve as the sole programmer responsible for all gameplay systems. Despite our part-time schedules, the team maintains strong collaboration and iterative development to steadily progress the project.

contributions.title: Gameplay Systems
contributions.description: Implemented all core gameplay systems as the sole programmer, including player movement, artifact interactions, puzzle mechanics, and dungeon navigation logic.

contributions.title: Artifact Mechanics
contributions.description: Designed and scripted the unique abilities of Ghost artifacts like Kealen, ensuring each interacts seamlessly with environmental puzzles and progression systems.

contributions.title: AI & Interactive Objects
contributions.description: Developed AI behavior and interactive object logic, integrating visual and audio feedback to create a responsive and immersive gameplay experience.

contributions.title: Project Architecture
contributions.description: Maintained the overall project structure in Unreal Engine 5.6, creating a scalable and robust foundation to support rapid iteration and level design.

photoboss9.title: 📸 Engineering Diary: Building a High-Performance Image Pipeline in PhotoBoss
photoboss9.summary: Refactoring the thumbnail system into a formal pipeline stage with Direct-to-Size decoding, smooth lerped progress reporting, and race-condition-safe UI batching.

page.title: Engineering Diary: Building a High-Performance Image Pipeline in PhotoBoss
page.introPara1: Over the past few commits, I’ve taken a deep dive into the engine room of PhotoBoss. My goal was simple but ambitious: transform a sluggish, deadlock-prone thumbnail system into a professional-grade, high-concurrency pipeline.
page.introPara2: Here’s a look at the "What," the "Why," and where I’m headed next.
page.sec1Para1: Before I started these changes, PhotoBoss was hitting three significant walls:
page.sec1List1: <strong1>The Deadlock Wall</strong1>: On large datasets, the UI would "hang" or freeze because the thumbnail system and the discovery engine were competing for resources without a unified controller.
page.sec1List2: <strong1>The Memory Wall</strong1>: I was loading full 20MP+ high-resolution images into memory just to generate a 150-pixel thumbnail. On modern cameras, this is a massive waste of CPU and RAM.
page.sec1List3: <strong1>The Shutdown Wall</strong1>: Stopping a scan often left "ghost" threads running, or forced me to shut down prematurely, losing the results already processed by the hashing engine.
page.sec2Para1: I replaced the fragmented <code1>ThumbnailProvider</code1> singleton with a formal <strong1>Thumbnail Generator Stage</strong1> integrated directly into the <code2>PipelineController</code2>.
page.sec2TitleSub1: The Unified Progress Bar (The UX Secret)
page.sec2ParaSub1_1: One of the most visible improvements is the new unified progress reporting system. Previously, the progress bar was jumpy and disconnected, flapping between scanning counts and processing counts.
page.sec2ParaSub1_2: I’ve consolidated these into a single linear journey. To make it feel truly premium, I didn't just hook up raw signals. Instead, I implemented a <strong1>Linear Interpolation (Lerping)</strong1> mechanism in the <code1>PipelineController</code1>.
page.sec2ParaSub1_3: Now, a 30ms timer drives the UI updates. If the processing engine suddenly finishes 500 hashes, the progress bar doesn't "snap" forward; it floats smoothly toward the target value:
page.sec2ParaSub1_4: This small change has a massive impact on UX. It makes the application feel stable and responsive, even when the underlying work is performed in jagged, multi-threaded bursts.
page.sec2ParaSub2_1: The biggest performance "win" was changing how I talk to the disk. Instead of:
page.sec2ParaSub2_2: <code1>File -> Load Full (Slow) -> Rotate Full (Very Slow) -> Scale Down</code1>
page.sec2ParaSub2_4: <code1>File -> Decode only the 150x150 pixels I need (Instant) -> Rotate tiny buffer -> Ready</code1>
page.sec2ParaSub2_5: By decoding only the sub-sampled pixels, I’ve reduced the work required per image by over <strong1>95%</strong1>. Rotating a small thumbnail is virtually free compared to rotating a 24-megapixel original.
page.sec2ParaSub3_1: To keep the UI responsive, I implemented a high-frequency population system in <code1>MainWindow</code1>. Instead of overwhelming the GUI thread with every single result, I now batch groups every 20ms. This keeps the frame rate high while allowing thumbnails to "zip" onto the screen in real-time.
page.sec2ParaSub4_1: In a multi-threaded system, sometimes the thumbnail would finish before the UI widget was even created! I added a local cache in the <code1>MainWindow</code1> that holds these "orphan" thumbnails until their corresponding UI element is birthed, ensuring zero missing images.
page.sec3Para1: PhotoBoss can now discover thousands of images, hash them for similarity, and populate the UI with thumbnails in a single fluid motion. The pipeline is stable, memory-efficient, and correctly drains its backlog during shutdown.
page.sec4Para1: I’m now shifting my focus toward user control and data management.
page.sec4ParaSub1: Currently, the "Start Scan" button is a one-way street. I’ll be implementing a <strong1>Stop/Interrupt</strong1> toggle. When a scan is running, the button will change to "Stop," allowing users to gracefully interrupt the pipeline. This requires careful signaling through all stages to stop work immediately without crashing.
page.sec4ParaSub2: Finding duplicates is only useful if you can act on them. I am building the logic to safely move files to the system trash or delete them directly from the UI, with integrated safety checks so you don't accidentally wipe your best shots.
page.sec4ParaSub3_1: Not every user wants the same thing. Some want exact bit-for-bit duplicates; others want to find photos taken a second apart.
page.sec4ParaSub3_2: <strong1>Exact Mode</strong1>: Tight thresholds for high-speed duplicate cleanup.
page.sec4ParaSub3_3: <strong1>Similar Mode</strong1>: Looser thresholds for grouping "near-miss" shots.
page.sec4ParaSub3_4: I'll be adding a UI for these engine parameters so you can tune the discovery to your library's needs.
page.outro: PhotoBoss is getting faster and smarter every day. Stay tuned as I move into the "Action" phase of the project!

photoboss8.title: 📸 Finding the Needle in the Haystack: Similarity Search & Grouping
photoboss8.summary: Implementing a similarity engine with weighted scoring (pHash, dHash, aHash) to group near-duplicate images, and refactoring the pipeline for pragmatism.

page.title: Finding the Needle in the Haystack: Similarity Search & Grouping
page.sec1Para1: We have a pipeline. We have a threaded worker pool. We have a robust, versioned database cache. The machine is built. Now it’s time to turn it on and do what we came here to do: <strong1>Find the duplicates.</strong1>
page.sec2Para1: I realised early on that a "One Pass Fits All" approach was wrong. Grouping exact byte-matches is easy (and objective). Grouping "similar" images is fuzzy (and subjective).
page.sec2ParaLayer1: First, I run a blazing fast pass using SHA-256. If two files have the same hash, they are the same file. Period. This cleared out about 4,000 files from my library instantly. These are the safe kills.
page.sec2ParaLayer2_1: This is where the math gets fun. I compare every image against every other image (using an indexing tree to avoid O(n^2) madness, of course).
page.sec2ParaLayer2_2: I defined a "Similarity Score" based on a weighted average of:
page.sec2ParaLayer2_3: Why mix them? Because <code1>pHash</code1> is great at surviving compression artifacts, but sometimes thinks a building looks like a book. <code2>aHash</code2> is great at color, but fails if you crop the image. Together, they form a jury. If the jury votes 90% "Yes", it's a match.
page.sec3Para1: I pressed "Scan". The pipeline roared to life. The cache hits flew by. And then, the "Groups Found" counter started ticking up.
page.sec3Para2: I opened the result viewer (which I finally styled in a sleek Dark Mode, because we aren't savages), and there they were.
page.sec3Para3: A photo of my dog from 2018. Next to it, a slightly smaller version labeled "Instagram Export". Next to that, a version with a sepia filter. <strong1>PhotoBoss knew they were the same.</strong1>
page.sec3Para4: But it wasn't perfect. It also decided that a picture of a grey wall was identical to a picture of a grey sky. And it grouped two completely different sunsets because the color palettes were mathematically identical.
page.sec3Para5: The similarity engine is like an over-eager puppy right now. It finds the ball, but sometimes it brings you a rock instead. I need to tighten the scoring thresholds and maybe introduce a "safety" check for edge cases.
page.imgAlt: Dark mode preview of the duplicate photo groups
page.sec4Para1: So, I can <em1>see</em1> the duplicates. But I can't easily kill them yet.
page.sec4Para2: I have a thumbnail view, but the actual "Delete" button is terrifyingly effective. I need to build a "Review & Confirm" workflow that feels safe to use on 15 years of memories. UX is hard, especially when the cost of a misclick is deleting your child's first steps.
page.sec4Para3: The engine is running. Now I just need to learn how to steer it.

photoboss7.title: 📸 Short-Circuiting Work: Introducing a Persistent Hash Cache
photoboss7.summary: Adding a versioned, persistent hash cache to avoid recomputing expensive perceptual hashes. This entry covers file identity, cache-first pipeline design, SQLite persistence, and how treating cached results as first-class pipeline outputs dramatically reduces unnecessary work.

page.title: Short-Circuiting Work: Introducing a Persistent Hash Cache
page.sec1Para1: In my last post, I designed the schema for my cache. Now it was time to wire it up.
page.sec1Para2: The "Identity" problem was the first hurdle. I needed a way to fingerprint a file without reading it. I settled on a composite key:
page.sec1Para3: Is it theoretically possible to modify a file while keeping its size and timestamp exactly the same? Yes. Is it likely to happen to my family vacation photos? No.
page.sec1Para4: For the database itself, I needed a schema that could handle multiple hash algorithms per file. I used a normalised approach:
page.sec1Code2:
-- Files table (The Identity)
CREATE TABLE files (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    path TEXT NOT NULL,
    size_bytes INTEGER NOT NULL,
    mtime_unix INTEGER NOT NULL,
    UNIQUE(path, size_bytes, mtime_unix)
);

-- Hashes table (The Expensive Work)
CREATE TABLE hashes (
    file_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    hash_method_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    value BLOB NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (file_id, hash_method_id)
);
page.sec1Para5: This is where the pipeline architecture I fought for in Part 2 really paid off. If I had written a spaghetti-code loop, adding caching would have been a nightmare of `if` statements.
page.sec1Para6: Instead, I just inserted a new node in the graph:
page.sec1Code3:
Scanner --> CacheLookup
CacheLookup -->|Hit| ResultAggregation
CacheLookup -->|Miss| DiskLoader
page.sec1Para7: The <code1>CacheLookup</code1> worker takes a file path, queries SQLite, and makes a decision. If the hash exists, it creates a "Job Done" signal and sends it straight to the finish line. The disk loader never even knows the file existed.
page.sec2Para1: I implemented the SQLite backend, set up the WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) for concurrency, and fired it up.
page.sec2Para2: <strong1>Run 1:</strong1> 45 minutes. (Expected. It had to hash everything from scratch).
page.sec2Para3: Then, the moment of truth. I closed the app. Re-opened it. And pointed it at the same 50,000 photos.
page.sec2Para5: I actually laughed out loud. The bottleneck had shifted entirely from "Parsing JPEGs" to "How fast can SQLite return rows?" (Answer: very fast).
page.sec3Para1: Because I versioned the hash methods in the database, I could now do something cool: I added a "Settings" dialog where I could toggle individual algorithms on and off.
page.sec3Para2: Want to only use MD5 for speed? Uncheck "Perceptual Hash." The pipeline adjusts instantly. Re-enable it? The system checks the cache, sees the missing values for <em1>that specific algorithm</em1>, and schedules jobs to compute only the missing data.
page.sec3Para3: It felt like I had finally tamed the beast. The infrastructure was solid. Now I could finally focus on the actual goal: finding the duplicates.

photoboss6.title: 📸 Designing a Persistent Hash Cache with SQLite
photoboss6.summary: Building a first-class cache stage using SQLite: identifying files, storing multiple hash algorithms, and injecting results back into the pipeline to skip redundant work.

page.title: Designing a Persistent Hash Cache with SQLite
page.sec1Para1: The core challenge of a cache is trust. If I ask the database <em1>"Do you know the hash for Photo.jpg?"</em1>, and it says <em2>"Yes,"</em2> I need to be 100% certain that <code1>Photo.jpg</code1> hasn't changed since that hash was calculated.
page.sec1Para2: But I can't read the file to check, because reading the file is the exact thing I'm trying to avoid.
page.sec1Para3: The solution is a proxy identity. I assume a file is unchanged if three values remain constant:
page.sec1Para4: Is it theoretically possible to modify a file while keeping its size and timestamp exactly the same? Yes. Is it likely to happen to my family vacation photos? No.
page.sec2Para1: Designing the database felt like putting together a puzzle. I didn't want a single flat table. I wanted a system that could handle multiple hash algorithms (MD5, pHash, BlockMean) for the same file.
page.sec2Para2: I settled on a normalised 3-table structure:
page.sec2List1: <strong1>Files Table:</strong1> Stores the Path, Size, and MTime. This is the "Key".
page.sec2List2: <strong1>Methods Table:</strong1> Stores the name of the algorithm (e.g., "pHash") and its <em1>Version</em1>.
page.sec2List3: <strong1>Hashes Table:</strong1> The glue. It links a File to a Method and stores the Blob data.
page.sec2Para3: This separation is powerful. If I update my pHash calculation code, I just bump the version number in the Methods table. The app will see the version mismatch and automatically re-compute the new hashes, while leaving the MD5 hashes untouched.
page.sec3Title: The Cache as a Pipeline Member
page.sec3Para1: The best part of this design? The cache isn't some side-car process. It’s just another stage in the pipeline.
page.sec3Para2: The <strong1>CacheLookup</strong1> stage sits right after the Scanner. It checks the DB. If it gets a hit, it sends the result directly to the UI, bypassing the heavy "loading" and "hashing" stages entirely. It feels like cheating.
page.imgAlt: SQLite schema for the hash cache

photoboss5.title: 📸 The Realisation: Recomputing Everything Is Wasteful
photoboss5.summary: Identifying the inefficiency of recomputing hashes for unchanged files, and defining caching requirements for persistence, invalidation, and multiple hash algorithms.

page.title: The Realisation: Recomputing Everything Is Wasteful
page.sec1Title: The Progress Bar that Never Ends
page.sec1Para1: My parallel pipeline was a thing of beauty. I could drag a folder of 10,000 images onto the window, and watch 32 CPU threads devour it. The fans would spin up, the progress bar would fly across the screen, and 45 seconds later, I had my results.
page.sec1Para2: Then I closed the app. And opened it again. And dragged the same folder in.
page.sec2Para1: It seems obvious in hindsight, but in the heat of "getting it working," I had ignored a fundamental truth: <strong1>My photos don't change.</strong1>
page.sec2Para2: A picture taken in 2012 and stored on my NAS hasn't been modified in a decade. Why was I spending expensive CPU cycles decoding and hashing it every single time I wanted to organise my library?
page.sec2Para3: I realised that for this tool to be actually <em1>usable</em1>—to be something I could open, tweak a filter, and close without feeling dread—it needed <strong1>Memory</strong1>.
page.sec3Para1: I grabbed a notebook (paper is still the best IDE) and sketched out what a caching system would actually look like. It wasn't just "save the results." It had strict requirements:
page.sec3List1: <strong1>Persistence:</strong1> It has to survive application restarts. (Goodbye, <code1>std::map</code1>).
page.sec3List2: <strong1>Invalidation:</strong1> If I <em1>do</em1> edit a photo, the cache must know instantly. Stale data is worse than no data.
page.sec3List3: <strong1>Versioning:</strong1> If I improve my hashing algorithm next week, I need a way to tell the database "throw away the old hashes, they are garbage now."
page.sec3List4: <strong1>Zero Config:</strong1> I didn't want to install a PostgreSQL server just to run my desktop app.
page.sec4Para1: I briefly considered using a massive JSON file or a custom binary format. Then I remembered I wanted to keep my sanity.
page.sec4Para2: SQLite was the only logical choice. It’s serverless, it’s single-file, and it has transactional integrity. If my app crashes halfway through writing a cache entry, the database won't get corrupted. Plus, Qt has excellent support for it via <code1>QSqlDatabase</code1>.
page.sec4Para3: The decision was made. Now I just had to design a schema that could identify a file uniquely without actually reading it.
page.imgAlt: Image processing pipeline showing cache lookup stage before hashing

photoboss4.title: 📸 Hashing Images: More Than Just Checksums
photoboss4.summary: Implementing both cryptographic and perceptual image hashing, separating IO, decoding, and computation into pipeline stages with worker pools for performance.

page.title: Hashing Images: More Than Just Checksums
page.sec1Para1: When you say "file comparison" to a programmer, they instinctively reach for MD5 or SHA-256. It’s reflex.
page.sec1Para2: So, that’s what I did. I built a lightning-fast pipeline that computed SHA-256 hashes for every file in my library. I was proud of it. It chewed through 100GB of photos in minutes.
page.sec1Para4: A single bit flip changes a SHA-256 hash completely. Opening a JPEG and saving it again (even at %100 quality) changes the hash. Resizing an image by 1 pixel changes the hash. My library was full of "duplicates" that were byte-distinct but visually identical.
page.sec2Para1: I needed a hash that behaved like a human eye. If I squint at a picture of a cat, and then squint at a smaller version of that same picture, they look the same.
page.sec2Para2: This led me to the world of <strong1>pHash</strong1> (Perceptual Hash) and <strong2>aHash</strong2> (Average Hash).
page.sec2List1: <strong1>aHash</strong1> breaks the image into an 8x8 grid of greyscale pixels and compares each pixel to the average brightness. It’s incredibly fast and great for finding resized copies.
page.sec2List2: <strong1>pHash</strong1> uses a Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) — the same math behind JPEG compression — to fingerprint the low-frequency structure of the image. It focuses on the "shape" of the image rather than the pixels.
page.sec3Para1: The catch? Calculating a pHash is expensive. You have to decode the full image, convert to greyscale, and run matrix math.
page.sec3Para2: My pipeline slowed to a crawl. The scanner was feeding file paths instantly, but the "Hasher" stage was choking on the CPU load.
page.sec3Para3: This forced me to rethink my worker pool. I couldn't just have one "Hasher" thread. I needed a swarm of them. I updated the pipeline to dynamically scale the worker pool based on the user's CPU core count (minus one, to keep the UI responsive).
page.sec3Para4: Now, looking at the Task Manager is satisfying: 100% utilisation across all cores, churning through memories at maximum speed.
page.imgAlt: Worker pool layout for image hashing stage

photoboss3.title: 📸 Learning Qt by Building: Threads, Signals, and Ownership
photoboss3.summary: A deep dive into Qt threading, signals/slots, and object ownership while building a responsive photo processing pipeline — including early mistakes and lessons learned.

page.title: Learning Qt by Building: Threads, Signals, and Ownership
page.sec1Para1: I chose Qt for this project because I wanted to learn it properly. I’ve dabbled before, but "dabbling" in Qt usually means "copy-pasting from StackOverflow until the window shows up."
page.sec1Para2: This time, I wanted to understand the machine. And oh boy, did the machine fight back.
page.sec2Para1: Coming from a generic C++ background, I assumed that if I had a pointer to an object, I could call methods on it.
page.sec2Para3: I spent three days debugging a race condition where my UI would update <em1>sometimes</em1>, but crash randomly. The culprit? <strong1>Thread Affinity</strong1>. In Qt, every <code1>QObject</code1> "lives" on a specific thread. If you call a method on it from another thread, you are breaking the law.
page.sec3Para1: The solution—and the moment the framework finally "clicked" for me—was fully embracing <strong1>Signals and Slots</strong1>.
page.sec3Para2: Instead of <code1>worker->doWork()</code1>, you emit <code2>requestWork()</code2>. Instead of the worker returning data, it emits <code3>workFinished(result)</code3>.
page.sec3Para3: It feels cumbersome at first. You’re writing boilerplate just to call a function. But then you realise what Qt is doing for you: <strong1>It’s marshaling the call across thread boundaries automatically.</strong1> Use a queued connection, and the data arrives safely on the receiver's thread, with no mutex locking required in your business logic.
page.sec4Para1: One specific trap I fell into:
page.sec4Para2: I learned the hard way that the <em1>constructor</em1> of <code1>MyWorker</code1> still runs on the main thread. If you allocate sub-objects or timers in the constructor, they stay on the main thread, while the worker itself moves. The result is a Frankenstein object straddling two threads.
page.sec4Para3: Fixing this required a strict pattern: Do setup in a <code1>start()</code1> slot, not the constructor.
page.sec4Para4: It was a painful week, but my pipeline is now completely lock-free, relying entirely on message passing. It’s cleaner, safer, and arguably more "Qt" than anything I’ve written before.
page.imgAlt: Diagram showing multiple hash workers working in parallel on disk I/O results

photoboss2.title: 📸 From Idea to Architecture: Designing a Scalable Image Processing Pipeline
photoboss2.summary: How I modeled a messy photo library as a parallel pipeline of scanning, decoding, hashing, and aggregation — enabling scalable and responsive image processing.

page.title: From Idea to Architecture: Designing a Scalable Image Processing Pipeline
page.sec1Para1: In my head, the logic was simple. Iterate through every folder, load every image, hash it, and check for duplicates. Easy, right?
page.sec1Para2: I wrote the first prototype as a single loop. It worked beautifully for a test folder of 100 images. Then I pointed it at my main library of 50,000+ photos.
page.sec1Para3: The UI froze immediately. The disk thrashing was audible from the next room. And when I finally killed the process, I realised I had no way to restart without beginning from zero.
page.sec2Para1: I realised that "processing an image" isn't a single atomic action. It’s actually a series of very different operations, each with its own specific bottleneck:
page.sec2Para2: By running these in a single sequence, I was forcing my fast CPU to wait for the slow disk, and then forcing the idle disk to wait for the busy CPU. It was the worst of both worlds.
page.sec3Para1: I threw out the single loop and redesigned the system as a <strong1>Pipeline</strong1>. Instead of one worker doing everything, I imagined a factory line.
page.sec3Para2: A <strong1>Scanner</strong1> thread runs ahead, finding files and tossing paths into a queue. A pool of <strong2>Loaders</strong2> grab paths, pull the data off the disk, and pass the raw buffers to a pool of <strong3>Decoders</strong3>. Finally, the <strong4>Hashers</strong4> do the math and send the results to the UI.
page.sec3Para3: This approach—Producer-Consumer queues connecting discrete stages—solved the responsiveness issue instantly. If the disk is slow, the hashers just pause. If the CPU is slammed, the scanners wait. The system naturally balances itself.
page.sec4Para1: It’s tempting to "just use threads" (<code1>std::async</code1> is right there!), but raw threading quickly leads to a tangled mess of mutexes and race conditions.
page.sec4Para2: By enforcing a strict pipeline structure, I didn't just get performance. I got <strong1>sanity</strong1>. Each stage has a single input and a single output. I can test the "Decoder" stage in isolation without needing a valid disk system. I can swap out the "Scanner" for a test harness.
page.sec4Para3: The architecture was set. Now I just had to implement it in Qt without shooting myself in the foot.
page.imgAlt: High-level image processing pipeline showing stages and data flow

photoboss1.title: 📸 The Problem Nobody Talks About: Photo Libraries at Scale
photoboss1.summary: Exploring the hidden chaos of multi-year photo libraries — exact duplicates, resized copies, messaging exports — and why typical deduplication tools fail.

page.title: The Problem Nobody Talks About: Photo Libraries at Scale
page.sec1Para1: It started innocent enough. A few folders of photos from a 2010 compact camera, dragged onto a laptop. Then came the smartphones. Then the backups of the smartphones. Then the backups of the laptops that held the backups of the smartphones.
page.sec1Para2: Fast forward 14 years, and my home server is groaning under the weight of a digital history that has become impossible to manage. We’re talking about terabytes of memories, but they’re buried under a sediment of redundancy.
page.sec1Para3: I recently opened a folder named <code1>/backup_2018_final_sorted</code1>, only to find it contained another folder called <code2>/old_laptop_backup</code2>, which contained a nearly identical copy of the first folder, but with slightly different file names.
page.sec1Para4: It wasn't just a storage problem anymore. It was an archaeology problem.
page.sec2Para1: My first thought, like any engineer, was: <em1>"I'll just write a script."</em1>
page.sec2Para2: I grabbed a standard deduplication tool, ran it for 12 hours, and... it found maybe 10% of the junk. Why? because <strong1>binary equality</strong1> is a fragile concept in the real world.
page.sec2List1: <strong1>The Resized Copy:</strong1> An image exported from Lightroom for Instagram is "different" to the computer, but identical to me.
page.sec2List2: <strong1>The Metadata Shift:</strong1> A file copied from Android to Windows often gets its EXIF data tumbled, changing its hash.
page.sec2List3: <strong1>The Messenger Compression:</strong1> That photo sent via WhatsApp? It’s a completely new file now, stripped of its soul (and pixels).
page.sec2Para3: I realised that standard tools view files as <em1>data</em1>, but I needed a tool that viewed them as <em2>images</em2>. I didn't need to know if <code1>img_123.jpg</code1> equalled <code2>img_123_copy.jpg</code2>. I needed to know if they <em3>looked the same</em3>.
page.sec3Para1: This frustration birthed <strong1>PhotoBoss</strong1> (a working title that stuck). I decided to treat this not as a quick script, but as a serious engineering challenge. I wanted to build a system that could ingest hundreds of thousands of images, fingerprint them perceptually, and help me make sense of the chaos.
page.sec3Para2: It was also the perfect excuse to finally sharpen my modern C++ skills and dive deep into the Qt framework — not just reading the docs, but fighting the battles of thread affinity, ownership, and custom models.
page.sec4Para1: Over the next few posts, I’m going to document the architecture of this thing. It’s been a journey of "naive" implementations that crashed my PC, discovery of perceptual hashing algorithms, and the eventual realization that I needed a persistent database to keep sanity.
page.sec4Para2: This isn't a tutorial on "How to write a deduplicator." It's a dev log of "How I over-engineered a solution to a problem I created for myself."
page.imgAlt: Messy photo library with duplicated directories

labyrinth5.title: 🧠 Building a Real Constraint Solver for the Labyrinth
labyrinth5.summary: Building a proper constraint-based reasoning system for the labyrinth generator — introducing a unified validation pipeline, tile feasibility evaluation, and the foundations of a real constraint solver.

page.title: Phase 4 — Building a Real Constraint Solver for the Labyrinth
page.sec1Para1: After several phases of visual output, shifting tiles, and gameplay mechanics, this week took a different turn. Instead of focusing on what the labyrinth <em1>looks</em1> like, the work shifted toward understanding how the generator <em2>thinks</em2>. This phase marked the beginning of a more deliberate approach to the problem — treating the Labyrinth as a <strong1>constraint satisfaction problem</strong1> rather than a random tile arranger.
page.sec1Para2: The goal? To create a robust system that can evaluate whether a tile placement is valid under a growing collection of rules, and eventually allow the labyrinth to be generated with intent, structure, and even difficulty.
page.sec2Para1: In Phase 3, the concept of modular constraints was introduced through the <code1>ILabyrinthConstraint</code1> interface. This was already a big improvement — adjacency checks, tile compatibility, and rotation validity were no longer hardcoded into the generator.
page.sec2Para2: But adding more constraints made the cracks show. Random tile placement works fine for simple rules, but becomes brittle as soon as additional structure is required. Before long, you end up with placement failures that are impossible to debug or reproduce.
page.sec2Para3: The solution was clear: move toward a proper constraint satisfaction model, where the generator evaluates <strong1>feasible candidates</strong1> rather than guessing until something fits.
page.sec3Para1: One of the first architectural improvements was the introduction of a dedicated <code1>ConstraintContext</code1> — a lightweight struct that carries all the information a constraint needs to evaluate a tile placement.
page.sec3Para2: This change reduced method signatures dramatically and made constraint logic more consistent. Each rule now receives a single context object instead of multiple raw parameters, which makes adding new constraints far easier.
page.sec4Para1: Another major addition was the creation of a single pipeline responsible for evaluating all active constraints. Instead of each constraint being checked in ad-hoc places, the generator now flows everything through a clear, composable validator.
page.sec4Para2: The generator can now not only determine whether a placement is valid, but also <strong1>which constraints failed</strong1>. This is invaluable for debugging, tweaking rules, or building editor tools later on.
page.sec5Para1: With the validation pipeline in place, the next step was to evaluate <em1>all</em1> possible combinations of tile types and rotations — selecting from only the candidates that satisfy all constraints.
page.sec5Para2: This effectively turns the generator into a simple constraint solver. Instead of hoping a tile fits, the system now understands <em1>which</em1> tiles fit and <em2>why</em2>.
page.sec5Para3: And because all logic happens before any GameObjects are spawned, the process remains lightweight and scalable.
page.sec6Title: 🎚️ Setting the Stage for Scored Generation
page.sec6Para1: With the feasibility system working, the next step will be to introduce <strong1>scoring</strong1> — the ability to choose the <em1>best</em1> tile rather than the first valid one. This paves the way for:
page.sec6List4: or even soft constraints with penalties
page.sec6Para2: But even without scoring, the generator is now significantly more flexible, stable, and predictable than earlier versions.
page.sec7Para1: Phase 4 represents a fundamental shift in how the labyrinth is built. The early stages were about rapid prototyping, visual feedback, and getting tiles on the board. This phase marks the transition from procedural enthusiasm to <strong1>intentional architecture</strong1>.
page.sec7Para2:
Before: Generation was random, reactive, and hard to control.
Now: Generation is rule-based, modular, and extensible.
page.sec7Para3:
Before: Gameplay and generation logic were tightly intertwined.
Now: The generator is a self-contained reasoning engine capable of supporting multiple gameplay contexts.
page.sec7Para4: This phase also laid the groundwork for more advanced features: dynamic difficulty, thematic tile clustering, or even procedural campaign progression. By reframing everything around constraints and rules, future expansion becomes a matter of <em1>composition</em1>, not refactoring.
page.sec7Para5: The labyrinth finally has a brain — next, we’ll teach it style.

labyrinth4.title: 🧠 From Random Chaos to Structured Generation
labyrinth4.summary: Refactoring the labyrinth generator into a modular, rule-driven system built on constraints.

page.title: Phase 3: From Random Chaos to Structured Generation — Evolving the Labyrinth System
page.sec1Para1: Following the progress in Phase 2, where the labyrinth grid first took shape through random tile placement and basic connection validation, the focus of this phase shifted toward structure, extensibility, and control. This was less about visual output, and more about engineering the system into something robust, testable, and expressive.
page.sec2Para1: The earlier implementation achieved visual results quickly — it could fill a grid with tile prefabs and apply rotations to approximate connectivity. However, it became clear that the underlying design was too rigid. The generator’s logic was buried inside monolithic methods that directly instantiated <code1>GameObjects</code1>, which made experimentation difficult. Each design tweak risked breaking the system because generation, validation, and presentation were tightly coupled.
page.sec3Para1: The first major architectural shift came with the introduction of the <code1>ILabyrinthConstraint</code1> interface — a lightweight abstraction that represents a rule applied during generation.
page.sec3Para2: Instead of hardcoding adjacency logic, the generator now queries a set of constraint objects that each implement a single validation rule. This approach allows constraints to be added, removed, or replaced without modifying the core generator.
page.sec3Para3: The simplest example is the <code1>ConnectionConstraint</code1>, which ensures that each tile aligns correctly with its neighbors’ open paths:
page.sec3Para4: With this in place, adjacency checks are no longer special-case logic inside the generator — they’re just one of many possible constraints. This unlocks powerful future flexibility: tile distribution rules, symmetry constraints, difficulty scaling, or even narrative layout logic can all be defined the same way.
page.sec4Para1: A second improvement came with the introduction of a dedicated entry point — the <code1>LabyrinthGeneratorAPI</code1>.
page.sec4Para2: Previously, generating a labyrinth required manually constructing the generator, adding constraints, and invoking its methods directly. That cluttered the gameplay code and made it harder to swap generation strategies later.
page.sec4Para3: The new API wraps this into a single clean call:
page.sec4Para4: This pattern — lightweight factory on top of a modular generator — strikes a strong balance between ease of use and extensibility.
page.sec5Para1: A key strength of the earlier implementation was that tile prefabs were never instantiated until the full grid was completed. This design already avoided one of Unity’s most common procedural pitfalls — the performance cost of constant creation and destruction cycles.
page.sec5Para2: However, even with that optimization, the process still treated visual tiles as the primary representation of the labyrinth. This phase introduced a conceptual shift: separating the logical grid from its visual layer.
page.sec5Para3: Now, the generator first operates on an abstract data structure of <code1>TileConnection</code1> masks. Each potential placement is validated purely in logic — applying constraints, rotations, and adjacency checks — before any GameObjects are spawned. Only once a tile type and rotation have been selected does the generator call the provided <code2>spawnTile</code2> delegate to create the prefab:
page.sec5Para4: This dramatically reduces overhead, allowing the generator to scale up to larger grids or more complex constraint sets without performance issues.
page.sec6Para1: On the gameplay side, the <code1>LabyrinthGrid</code1> <code2>MonoBehaviour</code2> was updated to integrate seamlessly with the new generation pipeline. It now uses the API directly, manages the spare tile mechanic, and handles shifting rows and columns with smooth coroutine-driven animation.
page.sec6Para2: A simple keyboard control scheme (<kbd1>W</kbd1>/<kbd2>A</kbd2>/<kbd3>S</kbd3>/<kbd4>D</kbd4>) triggers the grid shifts, and the system now tracks previous moves to prevent immediate reversals — a subtle but meaningful gameplay rule that enhances puzzle flow.
page.sec7Para1: Phase 3 marks a clear evolution from procedural enthusiasm to intentional architecture.
page.sec7List1:
Before: Generation was random, reactive, and hard to control.
Now: Generation is rule-based, modular, and extensible.
page.sec7List2:
Before: Gameplay and generation logic were intertwined.
Now: The generator is a self-contained system that can serve multiple gameplay contexts.
page.sec7Para2: This phase also laid the foundation for more advanced features — dynamic difficulty adjustment, thematic tile grouping, or even procedural level progression. By framing everything around constraints and rules, future expansion becomes a matter of composition, not refactoring.

labyrinth3.title: 🌀 Making the Labyrinth Shift!
labyrinth3.summary: Animating tile movement and grid shifting in Unity — bringing the Labyrinth to life with smooth transitions and interactive mechanics.

page.title: Making the Labyrinth Shift!
page.sec1Para1: Last time, we laid the groundwork for our digital version of <em1>The Crazy Labyrinth</em1> — building the concept of a grid, tiles, and a spare tile that can slide into the maze to change its layout.
page.sec1Para2: This week, we bring that concept to life. We now have a working Labyrinth grid in Unity, complete with tile movement, grid shifting, and a simple input system to control it — all in preparation for the procedural generation and gameplay phases ahead.
page.sec2Para1: Our objectives for this step were:
page.sec2List1: Generate a grid of random tile types (Straight, Bend, or T-junction)
page.sec2List2: Keep track of the spare tile (the “one out” tile that will be pushed in)
page.sec2List3: Allow inserting the spare tile from any valid side of the grid
page.sec2List4: Have the inserted tile “push” a row or column, ejecting the opposite tile
page.sec2List5: Animate tile movement to visually represent the shifting labyrinth
page.sec2List6: Set up basic keyboard controls for shifting (W, A, S, D)
page.sec2Para2: It’s a deceptively simple mechanic, but it’s the foundation that the whole labyrinth gameplay sits on.
page.sec3Para1: The core of the project is the <code1>LabyrinthGrid</code1> component, managing a 2D array of <code2>Tile</code2> objects and the spare tile, along with logic to insert and shift them around the board.
page.sec3Para2: We also introduced a lightweight <code1>GridPosition</code1> struct to represent coordinates — making it easier to reason about tile positions and equality checks without relying on raw integers.
page.sec4Para1: For player control, we’re using Unity’s Input System directly — just simple key bindings for now:
page.sec4Para2: When one of these is pressed, we insert the spare tile into the corresponding edge:
page.sec5Para1: When we insert a tile, we determine which direction the shift should occur — up, down, left, or right — and perform the array manipulation:
page.sec5Para2: This pattern applies for all directions, ejecting the opposite tile and updating the spare. To prevent undoing the last move, we track the previous insertion and disallow reversals.
page.sec6Para1: We now animate every tile when the labyrinth shifts, so it looks fluid and satisfying. Instead of running dozens of separate coroutines, we batch the updates into one smooth animation coroutine:
page.sec7Para1: Unity’s <code1>[ContextMenu]</code1> allows triggering functions from the Inspector — no runtime code required. We applied it to <code2>InitializeGrid()</code2>:
page.sec8List1: Randomly generates a full grid of tiles
page.sec8List2: Has a spare tile ready for insertion
page.sec8List3: Responds to keyboard input (W, A, S, D)
page.sec9Para1: In the next phase, we’ll focus on:
page.sec9List2: Adding visual insertion indicators (clickable entry points)
page.sec9List3: Tracking paths and connectivity between tiles
page.sec9Para2: Once these pieces are in place, we can add player tokens and objectives, transforming this demo into a playable Labyrinth game.

labyrinth2.title: 🌀 Designing a Dynamic Maze Tile System
labyrinth2.summary: Creating the core tile types, rotations, and grid structure for a procedural Labyrinth in Unity.

page.title: Designing a Dynamic Maze Tile System in Unity
page.sec1Para1: In the previous post, I introduced the idea of recreating <em1>Verrückte Labyrinth</em1> in Unity — a living, shifting maze that we can use to explore procedural generation.
page.sec1Para2: Today, we start building it for real. Phase 1 is all about data and representation — defining what a tile is, how it connects to its neighbors, and making it appear in the scene as a small piece of a larger, living system.
page.sec2List1: Three tile archetypes: Straight, Corner, and T-Junction
page.sec2List3: A simple grid of randomized tiles displayed in Unity
page.sec3Para1: The magic of the physical Labyrinth board is that each tile connects to others along its open paths. To capture that in code, we can use an enum with bit flags, one for each direction:
page.sec3Code1:
[Flags]
public enum TileConnection
{
    None  = 0,
    Up    = 1 << 0,
    Right = 1 << 1,
    Down  = 1 << 2,
    Left  = 1 << 3
}
page.sec3Para2: This lets us describe any tile shape compactly, for example:
page.sec3Code2:
// A corner piece connecting Up and Right
TileConnection.Up | TileConnection.Right
page.sec3Para4: …and easy to rotate — which we’ll handle next.
page.sec4Para1: Since each tile can be rotated 90° at a time, I wrote a simple helper to rotate the connection mask clockwise:
page.sec4Para2: Now every tile can easily adjust its connectivity when rotated in the scene.
page.sec5Para1: Each type of tile (Straight, Corner, T-Junction) can be represented by a <strong1>ScriptableObject</strong1> that defines its base shape and visual prefab. This way we can tweak them in the editor instead of hardcoding data.
page.sec6Para1: Each tile on the board is an instance of one of those TileTypes, with a rotation applied and a position in the grid:
page.sec7Para1: To quickly test the system, create a small script that spawns a random layout:
page.sec7Para2: When you press Play, you should see a 7×7 grid of randomly oriented tiles — your first procedural labyrinth taking shape.
page.sec7Para3: Here’s a quick look at what my prototype currently generates:
page.sec8Para1: It’s always satisfying to get that first visual confirmation that the system is working. Seeing the random grid appear in Unity makes it feel tangible — and more importantly, we now have the foundation to build on.
page.sec8Para2: Next time, I’ll be focusing on Phase 2: the grid logic — adding the classic Verrückte Labyrinth mechanic of pushing rows, tracking the spare tile, and watching the maze shift dynamically.

labyrinth.title: 🌀 Building a Living Labyrinth
labyrinth.summary: Exploring procedural maze generation inspired by Verrückte Labyrinth and turning board game mechanics into a Unity project.

page.title: Building a Living Labyrinth: Procedural Mazes Inspired by Verrückte Labyrinth
page.sec1Para1: Last night, I was playing <em1>Verrückte Labyrinth</em1> (also known as <em2>The aMAZEing Labyrinth</em2>) with my daughter. As we slid tiles around the board and watched new paths appear, I couldn’t stop thinking — this mechanic would be perfect for exploring procedural generation and tile-based systems in Unity.
page.sec1Para2: So, I’ve decided to turn that spark into a small side project (and blog series): <strong1>Building a Living Labyrinth</strong1> — a digital take on the board game’s ideas, built from the ground up with code, randomness, and a touch of design philosophy.
page.sec2Para1: At its heart, <em1>Verrückte Labyrinth</em1> is about structure and change. The maze exists as a grid of tiles, but every turn the shape of that maze can shift — entire rows move, new passages open, and others close. That mix of stability and chaos makes it a rich playground for procedural design.
page.sec2Para2: My goal for this series: <strong1>Create a Unity prototype</strong1> that procedurally generates and manipulates a tile-based labyrinth — inspired by the board game’s mechanics — and use it as a teaching tool for procedural generation.
page.sec3Title: 🧱 The Core Idea: Tiles and Connectivity
page.sec3Para1: The first concept to tackle is the tile system. Each tile in the game has a set of connections — pathways that can connect to other tiles. In the physical game, there are three archetypes:
page.sec3List1: <strong1>Straight Tile</strong1> — connects two opposite edges (│ or ─)
page.sec3List2: <strong1>Corner Tile</strong1> — connects two adjacent edges (┐, └, etc.)
page.sec3Para2: Each tile’s rotation determines how it fits into the labyrinth. So rather than hardcoding orientations, I’ll represent tiles by the sides they connect to — for example:
page.sec3Code:
[Flags]
public enum TileConnection
{
    None = 0,
    Up = 1 << 0,
    Right = 1 << 1,
    Down = 1 << 2,
    Left = 1 << 3
}

// Example: a corner connecting Up and Right
TileConnection.Up | TileConnection.Right
page.sec3Para3: This gives us a flexible, data-driven system — we can rotate, check connections, or even generate new tile types procedurally later.
page.sec4Para1: The game board is a square grid — typically 7×7 — with an extra tile off to the side. Here’s the fun part: that “spare” tile can be inserted into any movable row or column, pushing the others forward and ejecting a tile on the opposite side.
page.sec4Para2: That mechanic turns the maze into a living system — part grid, part queue — where some tiles are fixed in place, and others shift dynamically each turn.
page.sec5List1: <strong1>Tile representation:</strong1> Create prefabs or scriptable objects for each tile type.
page.sec5List2: <strong1>Grid management:</strong1> Build a simple GridManager to store tile states and positions.
page.sec5List3: <strong1>Manual shifting:</strong1> Let a player click to push tiles and animate the change.
page.sec5Para1: That’s enough to recreate the feeling of the physical labyrinth. Once that’s working, I can start introducing procedural level generation — randomizing layouts while maintaining valid paths.
page.sec6Para1: This is just the start. Here’s a rough roadmap for where I’d like to take this project:
page.sec6TableR1C3: Represent and visualize tiles with rotation and connections
page.sec6TableR4C3: Let a player (or AI) navigate the dynamic maze
page.sec6TableR5C3: New maze rules, animations, and maybe an online demo
page.sec7Para1: What excites me about <em1>Verrückte Labyrinth</em1> as a programming project is that it blends data structures, procedural generation, and player interaction. It’s simple enough to code in a weekend, but deep enough to study for months.
page.sec8Para1: In the next post, I’ll dive into the tile system itself — setting up the data model and rendering tiles dynamically in Unity. We’ll start with a simple grid of randomly oriented tiles, and by the end of it, you’ll be able to visualize the bones of your own living labyrinth.
page.sec8Para2: Stay tuned — and if you have ideas for features or ways to visualize the shifting mechanic, I’d love to hear them!
